title: US Strikes Iran Over Hormuz Drone Attack, Israel-Lebanon Announce Framework, Ceasefires Barely Hold Across Three Fronts
subhead: The US hit Iranian targets on Qeshm Island a day after Iran fired drones at a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz — the first American strikes on Iranian soil since the ceasefire. Meanwhile, Israel and Lebanon announced a peace framework even as fighting continues on the ground, and 500 ships remain stranded in a waterway neither side fully controls.

sections.heading: US Strikes Iran: The Ceasefire's First Military Test
sections.body:
The US military struck missile and drone storage facilities and coastal radar sites in Iran on Friday, including targets on Qeshm Island, in response to Iran's drone attack on a Singapore-flagged cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz a day earlier. CENTCOM called it a 'powerful response' to Iran's 'dangerous behavior.' Six land-based US aircraft hit four targets, a US official told CBS News. The strikes concluded about an hour after CENTCOM announced them.

President Trump said Iran launched four one-way attack drones at ships transiting the strait. Three were intercepted; the fourth struck the cargo ship Ever Lovely, damaging its bridge but causing no casualties. Trump called it 'a foolish violation of our Ceasefire Agreement.' Iran's parliamentary national security chief, Ebrahim Azizi, pushed back: 'This is not a violation of the ceasefire; it is ceasefire management,' he wrote on social media, adding that the Strait of Hormuz 'is governed by Iran, so: Respect the rules.'

VP JD Vance warned: 'If they have disagreements about how the MOU is being applied, they can pick up the phone. But violence will be met with violence.' Iran's foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, said 'every time a diplomatic solution is on the table, the US opts for a reckless military adventure.' The contradiction is stark: both sides say they want the deal to hold, but neither is willing to yield on who controls the strait.
sections.whyItMatters: These are the first US strikes on Iranian territory since the June 17 MOU. The ceasefire survived the exchange — barely — but the underlying dispute over who controls the Strait of Hormuz is unresolved and is being settled by force on the water.

sections.heading: Israel and Lebanon Announce a Peace Framework — But Nothing Stops on the Ground
sections.body:
On the same day as the Hormuz escalation, Israel and Lebanon announced what both sides described as a step toward peace. Lebanon's ambassador to the US, Nada Hamadeh, called it a move toward 'enabling our people to go back to their land and allowing all Lebanese to live in peace, security, and prosperity.' Netanyahu called it 'a great achievement for Israel' — then immediately qualified it: 'The most important thing, first and foremost, is that Israel will remain in the security zone in southern Lebanon until Hezbollah is disarmed and no longer poses a threat.'

That qualifier is the entire story. AP reports that Israel now controls roughly 60% of Gaza and has been expanding its occupation of southern Lebanon for months, capturing a Crusader fortress at Beaufort in its deepest incursion in 26 years. Hezbollah has kept up rocket fire into northern Israel. The April Lebanon ceasefire, like the Gaza and Iran ceasefires, has never fully held. Israeli forces continue ground operations and airstrikes; Hezbollah continues rocket launches. AP's headline puts it plainly: 'Fighting persists despite ceasefires in Gaza, Lebanon and Iran.'

Iran had conditioned reopening the Strait of Hormuz on Israel ending its Lebanon campaign. The framework announcement may be designed in part to unlock that pressure point. Whether it leads to an actual ceasefire or simply provides diplomatic cover for continued occupation remains an open question.
sections.whyItMatters: The Israel-Lebanon framework is diplomatically significant but substantively thin. Israel's insistence on staying in southern Lebanon until Hezbollah disarms mirrors conditions that have stalled every previous negotiation. If the framework doesn't produce a real ceasefire, it could undermine both the Lebanon track and the Iran-Hormuz talks that depend on it.

sections.heading: Hormuz: 500 Ships Still Trapped, IMO Evacuation Frozen
sections.body:
The International Maritime Organization halted its evacuation of stranded ships after the drone attack on Thursday. About 115 vessels managed to leave the Persian Gulf in recent days before the operation was paused, but approximately 500 ships — and more than 11,000 crew members — remain stuck, IMO secretary-general Arsenio Dominguez said Friday. The evacuation won't resume until safety guarantees are secured.

The numbers tell the story of a deal that hasn't yet delivered. Before the war, more than 130 vessels transited the strait daily. On Wednesday — the day before the drone strike — 78 ships passed through, the highest since the war began but still 40% below normal. After Iran's IRGC issued warnings that ships must use only Tehran-approved routes, at least two tankers reversed course, according to Lloyd's List Intelligence. Marine data firm Windward called it 'the first significant test' of the week-old deal's normalization: 'The pace of normalization has slowed.'

The routing dispute is fundamental. The MOU specifies toll-free passage for 60 days, and the US has promoted an alternative route hugging Oman's coast. Iran's newly created Persian Gulf Strait Authority insists vessels must use Iranian-designated routes or forfeit safe-passage guarantees — and has not ruled out charging tolls after the 60-day window. CENTCOM says it 'continues to provide safe passage coordination.' On the water, that coordination is being tested by drone fire and divergent maps.
sections.whyItMatters: The strait is the deal's proving ground. With 500 ships still trapped, the IMO evacuation paused, and Iran enforcing its own routing rules with armed drones, the gap between the diplomatic text and the operational reality is widening by the day.

sections.heading: Switzerland Talks: Progress Claims Meet Hardline Pushback
sections.body:
Vice President JD Vance flew to Switzerland for the first round of technical negotiations on Iran's nuclear program, telling reporters he was 'optimistic' about progress. The US delegation includes top negotiators Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff. Iran sent parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf, foreign minister Abbas Araghchi, and central bank and oil officials. Pakistani PM Shehbaz Sharif and army chief Field Marshal Asim Munir also traveled to Switzerland as mediators.

But the talks started badly. Iran canceled an earlier planned session because of escalating fighting in Lebanon. Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei said negotiations toward a final agreement will only begin once 'key commitments are upheld' — warning that if they are not, 'the memorandum of understanding as a whole will be jeopardized.' A senior Iranian parliament member, Ebrahim Rezaei, dismissed the 14-point MOU as a 'wish list' and warned Iran 'has its finger on the trigger.'

CSIS analysis notes that the nuclear file is the hardest part of the deal. The 2015 JCPOA took two years to negotiate; the MOU sets a 60-day window. Iran has signaled an unwillingness to compromise on key demands, and Trump is unlikely to resume the military campaign before the November midterms. Both sides have leverage; neither has urgency.
sections.whyItMatters: The Switzerland talks are the mechanism that is supposed to turn the MOU into a permanent agreement. But Iran's hardliners are openly hostile, the Lebanon question remains unresolved, and the 60-day clock is ticking on a negotiation that could take years.

sections.heading: NATO's 5% Pledge Meets Reality: From The Hague to Ankara
sections.body:
At last year's Hague summit, NATO's 32 members committed to spending 5% of GDP on defense and security by 2035 — 3.5% on core military capabilities and 1.5% on defense-related infrastructure. It was, as the Atlantic Council put it, a deal that 'dances to Trump's tune.' Now the alliance is preparing for its next summit, this time in Turkey, where secretary general Mark Rutte has vowed that members will 'defend every inch of our territory.' The 5% target replaced the 2% benchmark that eight members were still failing to meet as of 2025.

The gap between pledge and reality is wide. Spain, Belgium, and Canada have only recently announced plans to reach 2%. The 5% figure — which would require European allies and Canada to roughly double their current spending — remains aspirational for most. Meanwhile, the Iran war has demonstrated that European security assumptions need updating: US force reductions in Europe, the vulnerability of Gulf shipping lanes, and the demonstrated inability of European navies to secure trade routes without American support all point to a continent that has under-invested for decades.

The Ankara summit will also have to address the alliance's internal fractures from the Iran episode. Turkey, which has its own relationship with Tehran, has positioned itself as a potential mediator. The Atlantic Council identifies five priority outcomes for Ankara: translating the 5% pledge into concrete capability commitments, addressing alliance unity frayed by the Iran war, beginning to define Europe's role in Gulf security, and managing the continuing debate over Ukraine's membership path.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hague's 5% pledge was easy to sign. Paying for it — and building the capabilities it is supposed to fund — is the hard part. The Ankara summit will test whether NATO can turn a headline number into a fighting force, or whether the 5% target becomes the next 2%: a benchmark that most members treat as optional.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire survived its most serious test yet — an Iranian drone attack on a cargo ship followed by American strikes on Iranian soil — but it is fraying on the water and contested at the negotiating table. Iran calls the drone attack 'ceasefire management' and enforces its own routing rules; the US calls it a violation and bombs the enforcement infrastructure. Meanwhile, Israel and Lebanon announced a peace framework that changes nothing on the ground, 500 ships remain trapped in the strait, and the Switzerland nuclear talks opened with Iran's hardliners calling the whole deal a 'wish list.' Across three separate ceasefire arrangements — Iran, Gaza, Lebanon — the pattern is the same: agreements exist on paper, fighting continues on the ground, and no party has yet chosen to stop shooting over keeping whatever leverage they hold.
